intro: March 2001. The dot-com bubble just detonated. Amazon's stock has cratered from over $100 to single digits, Barron's ran a cover calling you 'Amazon.bomb,' and analysts are openly modeling your bankruptcy. You have never turned a profit. You run Amazon.

realOutcome.summary: Amazon survived the crash and became one of the most valuable companies on Earth. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: February 2000. The market is euphoric but you sense the top. You can raise $672M in convertible bonds now — expensive, dilutive, and it looks paranoid while everyone else is partying.

choices.text: Raise the $672M now, before the window slams shut.
choices.reveal: Bezos raised $672M in convertible notes weeks before the bubble burst. That war chest is the single reason Amazon didn't die in 2001 when the financing market froze solid.

choices.text: Stay lean. Raising cash now signals weakness.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The financing window closes in months and never reopens for unprofitable dot-coms. You run out of runway in 2001 like Pets.com and Webvan.

choices.text: Sell the whole company while the stock is still high.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cash out at the peak and Amazon becomes a footnote absorbed into a retailer that never builds AWS or Prime. A great personal trade, a historic mistake.

decisions.scenario: Your own retail buyers hate the idea, but you could open your product pages to third-party sellers — including rivals — letting them list right next to your own inventory.

choices.text: Open Marketplace. Let competitors sell on your pages.
choices.reveal: Amazon opened Marketplace to third parties in 2000-2001. It felt like cannibalization; it became half of all units sold and a massive high-margin engine.

choices.text: Keep it first-party only. Control the whole experience.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You stay a pure retailer carrying every ounce of inventory risk. eBay owns third-party commerce and Amazon grows a fraction as fast.

choices.text: Franchise the Amazon brand to other retailers.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You license your name and lose control of the customer experience that was your only real moat. The brand dilutes into noise.

decisions.scenario: Wall Street wants proof you can ever make money. You can grind toward your first-ever profit and obsess over free cash flow — or keep pouring everything into growth and dare them to doubt you.

choices.text: Drive to the first profit and worship free cash flow.
choices.reveal: Amazon posted its first-ever profit in Q4 2001 — a slim $5M — and Bezos reframed the whole company around free cash flow per share. It bought years of credibility.

choices.text: Ignore profit entirely, spend for pure growth.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: With the market in ruins and no profit in sight, lenders and investors walk. Growth you can't finance is just a countdown to zero.

choices.text: Force profitability overnight with brutal layoffs.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You hit a profit number by gutting the company, then can't invest in the infrastructure that becomes Prime and AWS. You optimized the wrong decade.

decisions.scenario: Your engineering org is a tangle. You could mandate that every team expose its work as a service through APIs — a huge, disruptive re-architecture with no obvious payoff — or leave the machine running.

choices.text: Mandate everything as APIs and internal services.
choices.reveal: Bezos' famous ~2002 'API mandate' forced every team to build service interfaces. It was the architectural seed that made Amazon Web Services possible a few years later.

choices.text: Keep the monolith. Focus every engineer on retail.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Retail ships a bit faster this quarter, but the plumbing for AWS never gets laid. You miss the highest-margin business you would ever have built.

choices.text: Treat infrastructure as a pure cost center to minimize.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You starve the very systems that could have become a cloud empire. Someone else invents the cloud and rents it back to you.

decisions.scenario: You want to offer unlimited two-day shipping for a flat $79/year. Your finance team is horrified — the math looks like lighting money on fire, and heavy shippers will abuse it.

choices.text: Launch Prime at $79 over finance's objections.
choices.reveal: Amazon launched Prime in February 2005 despite internal alarm. It became the most effective loyalty program in retail history, locking in the customers who now buy almost everything from you.

choices.text: Keep charging per-order shipping. It's real margin.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You protect shipping revenue and never build the habit loop that makes Amazon the default. Customers keep comparison shopping across the web.

choices.text: Raise the free-shipping order threshold instead.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A safer tweak that changes nothing structural. The membership flywheel that reshaped consumer behavior never spins up.

bands.line: You turned the crash into a launchpad. Legend episode: WON.
Alive and profitable, but no cloud, no Prime flywheel. A good retailer, not an empire.

endings.ctaLine: Amazon 300x'd after this fork. Today's agents flagged a company written off at a similar low →
